rcomplex 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Source: vignettes/rcomplex-tutorial.Rmd
rcomplex-tutorial.Rmd

This vignette demonstrates a complete rcomplex analysis using expression data from eight Pooideae grass species spanning four phylogenetic pairs, each containing one annual and one perennial species:

Species Code Life cycle Pair
Brachypodium distachyon BDIS Annual Brachypodium
Brachypodium sylvaticum BSYL Perennial Brachypodium
Hordeum vulgare HVUL Annual Hordeum
Hordeum jubatum HJUB Perennial Hordeum
Briza maxima BMAX Annual Briza
Briza media (crossmapped to BMAX) BMED Perennial Briza
Vulpia bromoides VBRO Annual Festuca/Vulpia
Festuca pratensis FPRA Perennial Festuca/Vulpia

The dataset contains the 1500 most highly expressed HOGs from VST-normalized RNA-seq from the “Live or let die?” project, stored as SummarizedExperiment objects.

1. Data preparation 

library(rcomplex)
library(SummarizedExperiment)
library(dplyr, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(tibble)

se_list <- readRDS(system.file("extdata", "pooideae_vignette.rds",
                                package = "rcomplex"))

We organise all per-species data in a single nested tibble. Each column accumulates one processing stage — the tribble defines species metadata, then mutate + map appends the SE objects, paralog reduction, and network construction as list-columns:

Paralog reduction

Multi-copy gene families often contain recent duplicates with nearly identical expression profiles. These inflate the combinatorial search space for clique detection without adding information. reduce_orthogroups() merges correlated paralogs within each HOG using Ward.D2 hierarchical clustering on Pearson correlation distance. Paralogs above the correlation threshold (default 0.7) are replaced by their averaged expression; subfunctionalized copies with distinct expression programs are preserved:

species_data <- tribble(
  ~species, ~trait,
  "BDIS",   "annual",
  "BSYL",   "perennial",
  "HVUL",   "annual",
  "HJUB",   "perennial",
  "BMAX",   "annual",
  "BMED",   "perennial",
  "VBRO",   "annual",
  "FPRA",   "perennial"
) |>
  mutate(
    se = se_list[species],
    reduced = map(se, \(s) {
      expr <- SummarizedExperiment::assay(s, 1)
      rd <- SummarizedExperiment::rowData(s)
      ortho_sp <- tibble(gene = rownames(s),
                         hog = as.character(rd$hog)) |>
        filter(!is.na(hog))
      reduce_orthogroups(expr, ortho_sp, gene_col = "gene",
                         cor_threshold = 0.7)
    })
  )

species_data |>
  select(species, reduced) |>
  pwalk(\(species, reduced)
    cat(species, ":", reduced$n_original, "->", reduced$n_reduced, "genes",
        "(", reduced$n_merged, "merged )\n"))

Network construction

Build co-expression networks from the reduced expression matrices using Mutual Rank (MR) normalization at 3% density:

species_data <- species_data |>
  mutate(network = map(reduced, \(r)
    compute_network(r$expr_matrix, norm_method = "MR", density = 0.03)))

# Named lists for functions that expect them
species  <- pull(species_data, species)
trait_vec <- deframe(select(species_data, species, trait))
networks <- deframe(select(species_data, species, network))

Ortholog extraction

Extract ortholog pairs from HOG annotations in rowData. After paralog reduction, gene names may have changed (merged representatives), so prepare_orthologs() maps gene names through each species’ reduction and deduplicates:

reductions <- deframe(select(species_data, species, reduced))
orthologs <- prepare_orthologs(se_list, reductions)

cat(nrow(orthologs), "ortholog pairs across",
    length(utils::combn(species, 2, simplify = FALSE)), "species pairs\n")

2. Pairwise comparison and HOG-level testing

For each of the C(8,2) = 28 species pairs, we run two analyses:

  1. Neighborhood comparison (compare_neighborhoods()): hypergeometric test for overlap of co-expression neighborhoods, producing per-pair p-values and fold-enrichment effect sizes.
  2. HOG-level permutation test (permutation_hog_test()): gene-identity permutation within each HOG. This is necessary because pair-level tests within a HOG are correlated (shared neighborhoods), making Fisher’s method or Storey q-values anti-conservative. The permutation p-value is exact regardless of the dependency structure.

With use_torch = TRUE, the fold-enrichment matrix is precomputed on GPU, reducing each permutation to fast table lookups.

The HOG-level q-values determine which edges are co-expressologs (conserved co-expression relationships):

n_cores <- min(4L, parallel::detectCores(logical = FALSE))

edges <- find_coexpressologs(networks, orthologs,
  method = "permutation", use_torch = TRUE, n_cores = n_cores)

cat(nrow(edges), "edges:", sum(edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved,",
    sum(edges$type == "ns"), "non-significant\n")

For quick exploration without GPU, the "analytical" method uses pair-level Storey q-values (less statistically rigorous for multi-copy HOGs, but much faster):

edges_analytical <- find_coexpressologs(networks, orthologs,
  method = "analytical")
cat("Analytical:", sum(edges_analytical$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges\n")
cat("Permutation:", sum(edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges\n")

3. Module-level analysis

To identify regulatory signatures of parallel life-cycle evolution, we detect modules in all eight species and compare across each of the four phylogenetic pairs. Module-level conservation reveals whether the same co-regulatory programs are used independently in annual lineages.

phylo_pairs <- tribble(
  ~pair_name,        ~annual, ~perennial,
  "Brachypodium",    "BDIS",  "BSYL",
  "Hordeum",         "HVUL",  "HJUB",
  "Briza",           "BMAX",  "BMED",
  "Festuca/Vulpia",  "VBRO",  "FPRA"
)

Testing for community structure (K = 1 null)

Before partitioning, we test whether each network has significant community structure at all. Passing a vector of resolutions triggers multi-resolution consensus clustering (Jeub et al., 2018) with a permutation-based K = 1 test: gene labels on a degree-preserving rewired null graph are shuffled and Leiden is re-run to build a null distribution of the spectral norm of the excess co-classification matrix. Early stopping terminates the permutation loop once the p-value is decisively above or below alpha_k1, so networks with clear structure typically complete in ~20 permutations rather than 100:

resolutions <- seq(0.25, 2.5, by = 0.25)

species_data <- species_data |>
  mutate(modules = map(network, \(net)
    detect_modules(net, method = "leiden",
                   resolution = resolutions,
                   objective_function = "modularity", seed = 42,
                   test_k1 = TRUE, n_perm_k1 = 100L,
                   max_consensus_iter = 1000L)))

modules <- deframe(select(species_data, species, modules))

species_data |>
  transmute(
    species, trait,
    lambda_obs       = map_dbl(modules, \(m) m$k1_test$lambda_obs),
    p_value          = map_dbl(modules, \(m) m$k1_test$p_value),
    has_structure    = map_lgl(modules, \(m) m$k1_test$has_structure),
    n_perm_completed = map_int(modules, \(m) m$k1_test$n_perm_completed),
    n_modules        = map_int(modules, \(m) m$n_modules)
  ) |>
  show_table(caption = "K = 1 community structure test (all species)")

The resolution scan shows how partition granularity varies across resolutions and how consecutive partitions agree (ARI):

show_table(modules$BDIS$resolution_scan, caption = "Resolution scan (BDIS)")

Module comparison across phylogenetic pairs

compare_modules_paired() wraps the full comparison pipeline into a single call: for each pair it runs Jaccard permutation (shuffling the ortholog mapping), classifies modules as conserved / species-specific, tags group-specificity from the group vector, and auto-coarsens the finer-grained partition when the module-count ratio exceeds 2-fold:

mod_results <- compare_modules_paired(
  modules, orthologs,
  pairs = phylo_pairs |> transmute(sp1 = annual, sp2 = perennial, pair_name),
  group = trait_vec,
  matched_scale = TRUE, coarsen_ratio = 2,
  method = "jaccard", n_cores = n_cores
)

show_table(mod_results$module_counts,
           caption = "Module count ratio per pair")
show_table(mod_results$summary,
           caption = "Module conservation: natural vs matched scale")

Do any HOGs consistently land in annual-specific modules across independent lineages? That would be strong evidence for parallel re-use of regulatory programs. tag_permutation() tests this formally: trait labels are shuffled across species (preserving marginal frequencies), and HOG recurrence is recomputed under each permuted labelling. A pair contributes HOGs only when exactly one of its species carries the target trait, so the null correctly accounts for the paired design:

parallel_test <- tag_permutation(
  mod_results$classification, modules, orthologs,
  pairs = phylo_pairs |> transmute(sp1 = annual, sp2 = perennial, pair_name),
  group = trait_vec, target_group = "annual",
  n_perm = 1000L, min_recurrence = 2L
)

parallel_test$recurrence_table |>
  count(n_pairs, name = "n_hogs") |>
  show_table(caption = "HOG recurrence in annual-specific modules across pairs")

cat("HOGs recurring in >= 2 pairs:", parallel_test$observed,
    "(permutation p =", sprintf("%.4f", parallel_test$p_value), ")\n")

Hub genes and trait conservation

Identify hub genes within each species’ modules, then classify each HOG by whether it acts as a hub across trait groups. Weighted degree (the default) is the recommended primary centrality for co-expression networks – it directly measures total co-expression strength to module neighbours, which is the standard definition of a hub gene. All three centrality measures (degree, betweenness, eigenvector) are reported regardless of which is chosen as primary.

To disentangle centrality from regulatory function, characterize_hubs() adds two metrics: bridge fraction (the share of a hub’s co-expression edges reaching genes in other modules – high values indicate a regulatory coordinator rather than an intra-module effector) and betweenness/degree ratio (importance for information flow relative to raw connectivity). When an expression matrix is provided, it also computes the coefficient of variation – regulators tend to show higher expression variability than their targets because they drive transcriptional changes.

species_data <- species_data |>
  mutate(hubs = pmap(list(species, modules, network, reduced),
    \(sp, mod, net, red)
      identify_module_hubs(mod, net, orthologs) |>
        characterize_hubs(mod, expr = red$expr_matrix)))

species_data |>
  transmute(species, trait,
            n_hubs  = map_int(hubs, \(h) sum(h$is_hub)),
            n_genes = map_int(hubs, nrow)) |>
  show_table(caption = "Hub genes per species")
hub_class <- classify_hub_conservation(
  deframe(select(species_data, species, hubs)),
  trait_vec,
  module_comparisons = mod_results$raw
)

hub_class |>
  filter(classification != "non_hub") |>
  count(classification) |>
  show_table(caption = "Hub conservation across all species")

The regulatory characterization helps separate potential regulators from highly-connected effectors. We aggregate bridge_fraction, bt_degree_ratio, and expression cv to the HOG level:

hub_regulatory <- species_data |>
  select(species, trait, hubs) |>
  unnest(hubs) |>
  filter(is_hub, !is.na(hog)) |>
  summarise(
    max_bridge = max(bridge_fraction, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_bt_deg = mean(bt_degree_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_cv = if (all(is.na(cv))) NA_real_ else mean(cv, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = hog
  )

hub_class |>
  filter(classification != "non_hub") |>
  left_join(hub_regulatory, by = "hog") |>
  select(hog, classification, n_species_hub, max_centrality,
         max_bridge, mean_bt_deg, mean_cv) |>
  arrange(desc(max_bridge), desc(mean_bt_deg)) |>
  show_table(caption = "Hub regulatory characterization (sorted by bridge fraction)")

HOGs that are hubs specifically in annual (or perennial) species – the annual_specific_hub, perennial_specific_hub, and rewired_hub classes – are candidates for parallel regulatory evolution. Ranking by bridge_fraction and bt_degree_ratio prioritizes those most likely to be true regulators:

hub_class |>
  filter(classification %in% c("annual_specific_hub",
                                "perennial_specific_hub",
                                "rewired_hub")) |>
  left_join(hub_regulatory, by = "hog") |>
  select(hog, classification, n_species_hub, hub_trait_groups,
         max_centrality, max_bridge, mean_bt_deg, mean_cv) |>
  arrange(desc(max_bridge)) |>
  show_table(caption = "Trait-specific and rewired hubs (ranked by regulatory potential)")

Co-expression partners of hub HOGs

After identifying trait-specific hubs, the natural question is: “Which other HOGs co-express with this hub across species?” get_coexpressed_hogs() resolves a HOG to its gene(s) in each species, extracts co-expression neighbors from the network, maps them back to HOGs, and aggregates across species. The coexpressed_traits column makes trait-group filtering straightforward:

candidate <- hub_class |>
  filter(classification %in% c("annual_specific_hub",
                                "perennial_specific_hub",
                                "rewired_hub", "conserved_hub")) |>
  slice_max(n_species_hub, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  pull(hog)

if (length(candidate) == 1) {
  partners <- get_coexpressed_hogs(
    candidate, networks, orthologs,
    species_trait = trait_vec, min_species = 2L,
    edges = edges
  )

  cat(candidate, ":", nrow(partners), "partner HOGs in >= 2 species —",
      sum(partners$coexpressed_traits == "annual", na.rm = TRUE), "annual-only,",
      sum(grepl(",", partners$coexpressed_traits), na.rm = TRUE), "cross-trait\n")

  partners |>
    slice_head(n = 20) |>
    show_table(caption = paste("Top co-expression partners of", candidate))
} else {
  cat("No trait-specific or conserved hubs found\n")
}

4. Edge robustness: density sweep

Network density controls how many edges are retained in the co-expression network. At 3% density, only the top 3% of all pairwise MR values pass the threshold – these are the strongest co-expression relationships.

We sweep over 15 density levels from 0.8x to 1.5x the baseline threshold, testing both directions:

  • Tighter (0.8x–0.95x, effective density ~2.4%–2.85%): does the signal survive a 5–20% reduction in edges? Surviving co-expressologs are strong, not marginal.
  • Looser (1.05x–1.5x, effective density ~3.15%–4.5%): is the signal specific to this density range, or does it persist as new edges are added? Cliques that only appear at very loose thresholds are likely noise from extra edges.

The 3% density choice is somewhat arbitrary. Sweeping a wide range around it reveals sensitivity to that choice – a truly robust co-expressolog should be present across a range of densities, not just at one particular cutoff.

The permutation pipeline is re-run at each level because network neighborhoods change with the threshold, altering the HOG-level q-values:

sweep <- density_sweep(networks, orthologs,
  multipliers = seq(0.8, 1.5, by = 0.05),
  method = "permutation", use_torch = TRUE, n_cores = n_cores)

# Edge survival across density levels
sweep |>
  transmute(
    Multiplier = paste0(multiplier, "x"),
    Eff_density = sprintf("%.2f%%", eff_density * 100),
    Conserved_edges = n_significant,
    Baseline = if_else(multiplier == 1, "***", "")
  ) |>
  show_table(caption = "Conserved edges across density levels")

The mean effective density masks species-level variation. Because each species has a different MR distribution shape, a uniform threshold multiplier produces different effective densities across species:

baseline_densities <- sweep |>
  filter(multiplier == 1) |>
  pull(species_densities) |>
  pluck(1)

tibble(
  species = names(baseline_densities),
  trait = trait_vec[names(baseline_densities)],
  eff_density = sprintf("%.2f%%", baseline_densities * 100)
) |>
  show_table(caption = "Per-species effective density at baseline (1.0x)")

The edge count may not decrease monotonically because the permutation test operates at the HOG level: tighter neighborhoods can sharpen the signal for some HOGs while eliminating it for others. Local fluctuations reflect the discrete nature of the permutation q-values. The interpretation is asymmetric: surviving tighter thresholds indicates strong signal, while surviving looser thresholds indicates specificity (not merely noise from additional edges).

Analytical alternative: clique_persistence(). The density sweep above is rigorous but expensive — it re-runs the full permutation pipeline at each threshold level. For a quick estimate, clique_persistence() computes the ratio of each co-expressolog edge’s MR value to the species’ density threshold. A persistence of 1.5 means the weakest supporting edge is 50% above threshold and would survive a 1.5x tightening. This is a principled analytical proxy: it correctly identifies the weakest link in each clique’s co-expressolog support. However, it cannot detect that tighter neighborhoods may change which HOGs reach significance in the permutation test, or that new cliques may form from HOGs that become significant at stricter thresholds. Use persistence for screening or when GPU is unavailable; use the full sweep for publication-quality robustness assessment.

5. Clique detection and structural stability

Phylogenetic structure of pairwise comparisons. The co-expressolog edge set contains C(8,2) = 28 species pairs, mixing three types: (1) focal pairs from the same genus (4 annual vs perennial sister pairs), (2) within-trait pairs comparing two annuals or two perennials from different genera (12 pairs), and (3) cross-trait/cross-genus pairs (12 pairs). Cliques built on all 28 pairs can span phylogenetically non-independent comparisons. classify_cliques() handles this by separating within-trait from cross-trait cliques in the waterfall, but cliques are not weighted by phylogenetic distance. For the current dataset this is acceptable: each genus contributes exactly one species per trait, limiting phylogenetic confounding.

Using edges from the baseline density level, we find cliques and test their structural stability via leave-k-out jackknife. The jackknife removes k species at a time and checks whether each clique’s gene assignments are preserved (purely structural, trait-agnostic). Trait annotations are added post-hoc:

# Use the baseline (1.0x) edges for clique detection and stability
robust_edges <- sweep |> filter(multiplier == 1) |> pull(edges) |> pluck(1)
cat("Using", sum(robust_edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved edges at",
    sprintf("%.2f%%",
            (sweep |> filter(multiplier == 1) |> pull(eff_density)) * 100),
    "density\n\n")

# Find cliques
cliques <- find_cliques(robust_edges, species, min_species = 2L)
cat(nrow(cliques), "cliques\n")

cliques |>
  count(n_species) |>
  show_table(pageLength = 10, caption = "Cliques by number of species")

Cliques with n_species = 2 are single conserved edges between one pair of species – the weakest form of co-expression conservation. The most compelling evidence comes from cliques spanning 3 or more species, as these require coordinated conservation across multiple independent lineages.

The conserved edges feeding into clique detection come from three types of species pair:

focal_set <- paste(phylo_pairs$annual, phylo_pairs$perennial)
robust_edges |>
  filter(type == "conserved") |>
  mutate(
    t1 = trait_vec[species1], t2 = trait_vec[species2],
    pair_type = case_when(
      paste(species1, species2) %in% focal_set |
        paste(species2, species1) %in% focal_set ~ "focal (sister pair)",
      t1 == t2 ~ "within-trait",
      TRUE ~ "cross-trait/cross-genus"
    )
  ) |>
  count(pair_type) |>
  show_table(caption = "Conserved edges by pair type")
# Jackknife stability (all k from 1 to N-2)
max_k <- length(species) - 2L
stab <- clique_stability(robust_edges, species,
                          species_trait = trait_vec,
                          all_species = species,
                          full_cliques = cliques,
                          max_k = max_k)
cat("Cliques tested:", length(stab$stability_class), "\n")
cat("Structurally stable at k=1:", sum(stab$stability_class >= 1L), "\n")

tibble(stability_class = stab$stability_class) |>
  mutate(stability_class = factor(stability_class, levels = 0:max_k)) |>
  count(stability_class, .drop = FALSE) |>
  show_table(pageLength = 10, caption = "Stability class distribution")

6. HOG classification with robustness ranking

Classify each HOG by its conservation pattern, then rank within each category by robustness. The waterfall assigns each HOG to exactly one class: complete > partial > differentiated > trait_specific > unclassified.

result <- classify_cliques(robust_edges, species, trait_vec,
                            min_species = 2L,
                            stability = stab)

result |>
  count(classification) |>
  show_table(pageLength = 5, caption = "HOG classification summary")

Restricting to cliques spanning 3 or more species (excluding single-edge 2-species signals) gives a clearer picture of multi-lineage conservation:

result |>
  filter(n_species >= 3 | is.na(n_species)) |>
  count(classification) |>
  show_table(pageLength = 5,
             caption = "HOG classification (cliques >= 3 species only)")
  • complete: all 8 species form a clique – deeply conserved across all Pooideae
  • partial: clique spans both annual and perennial species, but not all 8
  • differentiated: annual species have a clique AND perennial species have a clique, but no cross-group conservation – lineage-specific co-expression programs
  • trait_specific: only annuals or only perennials have a clique
  • unclassified: no conservation signal detected

The robustness ranking comes from combining density sweep survival with stability class. HOGs classified at a stricter density level with high stability class are the strongest findings:

# Count how many density levels each HOG's edges survive
hog_survival <- sweep |>
  transmute(multiplier,
            hog = map(edges, \(e)
              e |> filter(type == "conserved") |> distinct(hog) |> pull(hog))) |>
  unnest(hog) |>
  count(hog, name = "n_densities_survived")

result <- result |>
  left_join(hog_survival, by = "hog") |>
  mutate(n_densities_survived = replace_na(n_densities_survived, 0L))

# Annotate trait composition from the best clique per HOG
hog_traits <- cliques |>
  group_by(hog) |>
  slice_min(mean_q, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_longer(all_of(species), names_to = "sp", values_to = "gene") |>
  filter(!is.na(gene)) |>
  summarise(
    n_annual = sum(trait_vec[sp] == "annual"),
    n_perennial = sum(trait_vec[sp] == "perennial"),
    .by = hog
  )

result <- left_join(result, hog_traits, by = "hog")

# Show ranked HOGs per category
show_cols <- c("hog", "n_species", "n_annual", "n_perennial",
               "best_mean_q", "stability_class", "n_densities_survived")
show_cols <- intersect(show_cols, names(result))

Every clique now has a stability_class – the highest k at which it survives ALL species-removal subsets. This is a purely structural measure. Trait annotations (from the traits column in the stability output) are orthogonal: they describe which life-cycle groups each clique spans, not whether the clique is robust.

Partial 

show_classification(result, "partial", show_cols,
                    "Partial HOGs (cross-trait cliques)")

Trait-specific 

show_classification(result, "trait_specific", show_cols,
                    "Trait-specific HOGs (one lineage only)")

Differentiated 

show_classification(result, "differentiated", show_cols,
                    "Differentiated HOGs (within-group conservation, cross-group divergence)")

Complete 

show_classification(result, "complete", show_cols,
                    "Complete HOGs (all 8 species)")

Clique robustness diagnostics

The density sweep and jackknife stability above assess whether cliques survive threshold changes and species removal. Several additional diagnostics quantify clique quality at a finer grain.

Edge-level quality metrics. find_cliques() now returns three columns alongside each clique: intensity (Onnela intensity – the geometric mean of 1 - q.value across present edges), coherence (intensity divided by the arithmetic mean, equal to 1 when all edge weights are equal), and min_effect_size (the bottleneck enrichment across present edges). High intensity with high coherence indicates uniformly strong conservation; high intensity with low coherence flags cliques carried by one or two dominant edges. The cost_weights parameter controls gene-assignment ranking: the default c(q = 1, effect = 0) minimises mean q-value, while c(q = 1, effect = 1) uses a composite cost q * mean_q - effect * mean_effect that also rewards large effect sizes:

# Intensity and coherence are already in cliques from find_cliques()
cliques |>
  select(hog, n_species, mean_q, intensity, coherence, min_effect_size) |>
  filter(n_species >= 3) |>
  arrange(desc(intensity)) |>
  head(20) |>
  show_table(caption = "Top cliques by Onnela intensity (>= 3 species)")

# Composite cost: rank gene assignments by both q-value and effect size
cliques_composite <- find_cliques(robust_edges, species, min_species = 2L,
                                   cost_weights = c(q = 1, effect = 0.5))

Jaccard overlap in edge output. comparison_to_edges() (and therefore find_coexpressologs()) now includes a jaccard column: the geometric mean of the two directional Jaccard indices of neighborhood overlap. This complements the hypergeometric q-value with a non-parametric measure of neighborhood similarity (range 0–1). Large effect size with low Jaccard suggests enrichment driven by a small shared subset rather than broad neighborhood overlap:

robust_edges |>
  filter(type == "conserved") |>
  select(hog, gene1, gene2, species1, species2, q.value, effect_size, jaccard) |>
  arrange(desc(jaccard)) |>
  head(20) |>
  show_table(caption = "Top conserved edges by Jaccard overlap")

Birth/death persistence. clique_threshold_sweep() returns a $persistence element with one row per baseline clique. Each row records the birth (lowest multiplier where the clique exists), death (first multiplier above birth where it disappears), and persistence (death minus birth). A persistence of NA means the clique survived all tested multipliers. Unlike the density sweep survival count, persistence captures the continuous range of thresholds over which each clique lives:

sweep_result <- clique_threshold_sweep(
  cliques, species, networks, orthologs,
  multipliers = c(1.5, 2, 3, 5, 10),
  min_species = 2L, n_cores = n_cores
)

sweep_result$persistence |>
  arrange(desc(persistence)) |>
  head(20) |>
  show_table(caption = "Clique birth/death persistence")

Perturbation test. clique_perturbation_test() adds Gaussian noise to MR scores in each species network, re-thresholds, re-runs the comparison-to-cliques pipeline, and measures how often each baseline clique survives (Jaccard >= threshold to its perturbed counterpart). This tests robustness to measurement noise without recomputing correlations from scratch. Cliques with high survival_rate are insensitive to small perturbations in edge weights:

pert <- clique_perturbation_test(
  cliques, species, networks, orthologs,
  n_boot = 100, noise_sd = 0.1,
  min_species = 2L, jaccard_threshold = 0.5,
  n_cores = n_cores, seed = 42
)

pert |>
  arrange(desc(survival_rate)) |>
  head(20) |>
  show_table(caption = "Clique perturbation survival (100 bootstrap iterations)")

Intensity Z-score. clique_intensity_test() builds a null distribution by permuting the ortholog mapping (globally shuffling Species2 genes across all rows, destroying both the specific mapping and within-HOG gene grouping). For each permutation, cliques are re-detected and the intensity of the best-matching clique (by Jaccard) is recorded. The Z-score and empirical p-value test whether the observed gene-to-gene correspondence produces stronger co-expression conservation than random mappings:

intens <- clique_intensity_test(
  cliques, species, networks, orthologs,
  n_perm = 500, min_species = 2L,
  n_cores = n_cores, seed = 42, edges = robust_edges
)

intens |>
  filter(p_value < 0.05) |>
  arrange(desc(z_score)) |>
  head(20) |>
  show_table(caption = "Cliques with significantly elevated intensity (p < 0.05)")

Together, these diagnostics address complementary robustness questions: density sweep tests threshold sensitivity, jackknife tests species-removal stability, perturbation tests measurement noise, and the intensity Z-score tests whether the ortholog mapping itself drives the signal. Cliques passing all four are the strongest candidates for biologically meaningful co-expression conservation.

7. Biological interpretation

The central question is whether independent annual Pooideae lineages draw on a shared, latent regulatory architecture inherited from perennial ancestors. The analyses above provide several lines of evidence:

n_complete <- sum(result$classification == "complete", na.rm = TRUE)
n_partial <- sum(result$classification == "partial", na.rm = TRUE)
n_differentiated <- sum(result$classification == "differentiated", na.rm = TRUE)
n_trait_specific <- sum(result$classification == "trait_specific", na.rm = TRUE)

# Robust candidates: survive >= half the density levels and stability >= 1
n_robust <- sum(
  result$n_densities_survived >= ceiling(nrow(sweep) / 2) &
    !is.na(result$stability_class) & result$stability_class >= 1L,
  na.rm = TRUE
)

cat("Complete (all 8 species):", n_complete, "\n")
cat("Partial (cross-trait, < 8):", n_partial, "\n")
cat("Differentiated (independent annual + perennial cliques):",
    n_differentiated, "\n")
cat("Trait-specific (one lineage only):", n_trait_specific, "\n")
cat("Robust candidates (>= 50% density survival + stability >= 1):",
    n_robust, "\n")
if (exists("parallel_test")) {
  cat("\nParallel module signal: ", parallel_test$observed,
      " HOGs recur in annual-specific modules across >= 2 pairs (p = ",
      sprintf("%.4f", parallel_test$p_value), ")\n", sep = "")
}

Differentiated HOGs are the strongest candidates for regulatory divergence: the same ortholog group has co-expression relationships in both annuals and perennials, but these programs are incompatible (no cross-group clique). This pattern is consistent with a shared ancestral regulatory architecture that has been independently remodelled in each annual lineage.

Trait-specific HOGs (annual-only or perennial-only cliques) may encode lineage-specific regulatory modules. The formal permutation test above addresses whether annual-specific modules share HOGs across independent lineages more than expected by chance. A significant result supports the hypothesis that annuality draws on a shared regulatory toolkit rather than arising from independent de novo rewiring in each lineage.

Robustness ranking combines density sweep survival with jackknife stability class. HOGs classified at tighter density levels with high stability represent the strongest findings for experimental follow-up.

Regulatory characterization helps prioritize among hub candidates. Hubs with high bridge_fraction connect across modules, a hallmark of regulatory coordinators (TFs, signalling genes). High bt_degree_ratio indicates importance for information flow relative to raw connectivity. High expression cv suggests the gene drives transcriptional changes rather than passively responding. Candidates scoring high on all three metrics are the strongest regulatory hub candidates, especially when they also carry known DNA-binding or signalling domains (testable via an annotation overlay with PlantTFDB or InterPro).

Caveats. Co-expression conservation is necessary but not sufficient evidence for regulatory conservation. Shared co-expression could reflect shared ancestry (phylogenetic inertia) rather than convergent regulatory function. The paired phylogenetic design partially controls for this – each pair is an independent comparison within a genus – but formal ancestral state reconstruction of co-expression networks would be needed to fully distinguish these scenarios. The candidate HOGs identified here should be validated with functional annotation (GO enrichment, transcription factor binding motifs) and, where available, tissue- or stage-specific expression data.

Session info 

sessionInfo()

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
